agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A feature request to integrate Warehouse Management (Context A) and Sales/Order Management (Context B) by blocking orders when stock is insufficient and showing real-time stock availability. This is a classic cross-bounded-context coupling request that fundamentally changes the autonomy of both contexts.","bounded_context":"Cross-context integration between Warehouse Management and Sales/Order Management. Neither context currently owns this interaction — a new integration boundary is being introduced.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"StockLevel","definition":"An integer (>= 0) representing the current quantity of a Product available in the warehouse. Owned by Context A (Warehouse Management)."},{"term":"OrderLine","definition":"A line item on an Order referencing a Product by ID and a desired Quantity. Owned by Context B (Sales/Order Management)."},{"term":"Stock Reservation","definition":"A temporary claim on warehouse stock made at order placement time, reducing available-to-promise quantity without yet reducing physical StockLevel. Does NOT yet exist in either context — must be introduced."},{"term":"Available-to-Promise (ATP)","definition":"StockLevel minus all outstanding (unshipped) Stock Reservations. The quantity actually available for new orders. A derived concept that lives at the integration boundary."},{"term":"Stock Available Indicator","definition":"A read-model projection of ATP shown to the sales team. Eventually consistent — NOT a guarantee of reservability."},{"term":"Order Blocked","definition":"A new Order state indicating the order could not be accepted due to insufficient stock at the time of placement."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"StockLevel in Context A cannot go below zero.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a physical constraint — you cannot ship items that do not exist. Violating this corrupts warehouse state.","testable_assertion":"For any Product, StockLevel >= 0 at all times, including under concurrent operations."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"An Order must be blocked (not rejected/deleted — blocked) if the Available-to-Promise quantity for any of its OrderLines is insufficient at the time of placement.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy. A VP could decide to allow backorders, partial fulfillment, or waitlisting instead of blocking. The blocking threshold is a policy choice.","testable_assertion":"Given Product X has ATP of 5, when an Order is placed with an OrderLine for Product X qty 10, then the Order enters 'Blocked' state."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Stock Reservations must be used to track committed-but-not-yet-picked quantities. ATP = StockLevel - sum(active Reservations).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without reservations, two concurrent orders could each see StockLevel=1 and both succeed, violating BR-1 downstream at pick time. The reservation is what prevents overselling — it is a data consistency constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given StockLevel=10 and existing reservations totaling 7, ATP must equal 3. A new order for qty 4 must be blocked."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"The 'stock available' indicator shown to the sales team is eventually consistent and must NOT be treated as a guarantee of order acceptance.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If the UI indicator is treated as a promise, the system lies to users under concurrency. The indicator is a read-model projection; the reservation write-path is the source of truth. Treating a stale read as authoritative corrupts the trust model.","testable_assertion":"The stock indicator may show 'available' and an order placed moments later may still be blocked. No business logic may depend on the indicator value for order acceptance."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Context B (Sales) must NOT directly read or write Context A's StockLevel. Integration must occur via an anti-corruption layer or domain events.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Direct coupling between bounded contexts destroys their independent evolvability and creates a distributed data integrity problem. If Sales directly queries StockLevel, it bypasses reservation logic and creates race conditions.","testable_assertion":"No code path in Context B directly queries or mutates the StockLevel aggregate in Context A. All stock information flows through a defined integration contract (events or an explicit API)."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Stock Reservations must have an expiry or cancellation mechanism. Reservations for orders that are never fulfilled must eventually release stock.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The expiry window is a business policy (e.g., 30 minutes, 24 hours), but the existence of some release mechanism is essential to prevent permanent stock leakage. A VP could change the timeout but not remove it entirely.","testable_assertion":"Given a Stock Reservation created at time T with expiry window W, if the order is not confirmed/shipped by T+W, the reservation is released and ATP increases accordingly."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A Product with StockLevel=10 and no existing reservations","when":"A customer places an Order with an OrderLine for that Product, qty=5","then":"A Stock Reservation of 5 is created, ATP drops to 5, and the Order proceeds to its normal flow (not blocked)"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A Product with StockLevel=10 and existing reservations totaling 8 (ATP=2)","when":"A customer places an Order with an OrderLine for that Product, qty=3","then":"The Order is marked as 'Blocked' with a reason referencing insufficient stock, and no reservation is created"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"Two customers simultaneously place orders each requesting qty=6, and StockLevel=10 with no existing reservations","when":"Both orders are processed","then":"Exactly one order succeeds (reservation created, ATP drops to 4) and the other is blocked. StockLevel never goes negative. No overselling occurs."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A Product with StockLevel=10 and no reservations","when":"The sales team views the product in their UI","then":"A 'stock available' indicator shows a positive availability (may be slightly stale but reflects last-known ATP)"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A Stock Reservation that has exceeded its expiry window","when":"The expiry mechanism runs","then":"The reservation is released, ATP increases, and the associated order is transitioned to an appropriate state (e.g., cancelled or re-queued for review)"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"An order is blocked due to insufficient stock","when":"New stock is received from a supplier, increasing StockLevel","then":"Previously blocked orders can be retried or automatically unblocked (policy decision), but must still re-check ATP at unblock time"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"Context B is deployed or running","when":"Any stock-related query or command is executed","then":"No direct database access or direct aggregate access to Context A occurs. All integration flows through the defined contract (events, API, or shared kernel)."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Order contains multiple OrderLines spanning different products, some with sufficient ATP and some without","expected_behavior":"The entire Order must be blocked if ANY OrderLine has insufficient ATP. Partial acceptance of an order creates fulfillment chaos. This is an all-or-nothing reservation. (Note: partial fulfillment is a separate, future policy decision that must be explicitly designed.)"},{"scenario":"Stock is received (StockLevel increases) at the exact moment an order is being validated","expected_behavior":"The reservation mechanism must be the single arbiter. The order either gets a reservation or it doesn't. Race conditions resolved by the reservation write, not by read-time stock checks."},{"scenario":"A product is referenced in an OrderLine but does not exist in Context A","expected_behavior":"This is a data integrity issue. The order should be blocked with a clear error — 'unknown product in warehouse context.' This must not silently succeed or throw an unhandled exception."},{"scenario":"Reservation expiry fires but the order has already been shipped/fulfilled","expected_behavior":"The expiry mechanism must check order state before releasing. If the order is already fulfilled, the reservation should be marked as consumed, not released."},{"scenario":"StockLevel is updated to 0 by a manual warehouse adjustment while reservations exist","expected_behavior":"ATP can become negative in a logical sense (more reserved than available). The system must handle this gracefully — existing reservations are honored on a first-come basis at pick time, and new orders are blocked. The warehouse team must be alerted to the discrepancy."},{"scenario":"High-frequency stock indicator polling by many sales users","expected_behavior":"The read model for the indicator must be a separate projection (CQRS pattern). It must not load the write-side aggregates or reservation tables directly, as this would degrade write-path performance under read load."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Blocking orders on insufficient stock prevents overselling, which eliminates costly backorder recovery (customer compensation, expedited shipping, reputation damage). However, false blocks due to stale data or overly aggressive reservation expiry could cause lost sales. The reservation expiry window is a direct financial lever — too short loses legitimate orders, too long locks up inventory.","operational":"Introduces significant operational complexity: a new reservation lifecycle must be managed, expiry jobs must be monitored, and the integration contract between contexts must be maintained by both teams. The 'stock available' indicator adds a read-model that must be kept reasonably fresh — stale data erodes sales team trust. Incident response now spans two contexts.","user":"Sales team gains visibility into stock (positive). However, the eventually-consistent indicator WILL sometimes show 'available' when stock is actually committed — this will cause frustration if not communicated clearly. Customers whose orders are blocked need a clear, actionable status — not a silent failure.","risk":"The highest risk is introducing tight temporal coupling between Context A and B. If the warehouse system is slow or down, order placement is blocked entirely — this turns a warehouse outage into a sales outage. A circuit breaker or fallback policy (e.g., accept orders optimistically during warehouse downtime) must be designed. Without it, availability of the sales system is now bounded by the warehouse system's availability.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Sales Team","impact":"Gains real-time stock visibility (positive), but must be trained that the indicator is advisory, not a guarantee. Blocked orders will increase their support workload."},{"group":"Warehouse Team","impact":"Now responsible for a system whose reservation accuracy directly affects sales. Stock adjustments (cycle counts, damage write-offs) now have downstream effects on order acceptance. Increased operational accountability."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Orders may be blocked rather than accepted-then-backlogged. This is arguably better (honest) but feels worse (rejection vs. hope). Communication of blocked status and next steps is critical."},{"group":"Engineering / Platform Team","impact":"Must build and maintain the integration layer, reservation system, read-model projection, and expiry mechanism. This is a non-trivial distributed systems problem with consistency implications."},{"group":"Finance / Revenue Team","impact":"Overselling costs decrease. But overly conservative blocking could reduce order conversion rate. Needs monitoring of block rate vs. actual stock-out rate to tune policies."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The feature request contains a fundamental ambiguity (synchronous blocking vs. eventual consistency) and a likely contradiction (real-time indicator vs. bounded-context autonomy). The spec says 'automatically block' (implying synchronous coupling) and 'real-time indicator' (implying direct reads), but both violate bounded-context boundaries and introduce availability coupling. Without clarity on the acceptable consistency window, fallback behavior during outages, and whether partial fulfillment is in scope, the acceptance criteria above represent my best interpretation but could be materially wrong.","high_confidence_areas":["StockLevel >= 0 is an inviolable invariant","Direct cross-context coupling must be avoided","A reservation concept is necessary to prevent overselling","The stock indicator must be eventually consistent, not strongly consistent"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether 'block' means synchronous rejection or asynchronous state transition","The acceptable staleness window for the stock indicator","Whether partial order fulfillment is in scope","Fallback behavior when the warehouse context is unavailable","Who owns the reservation aggregate — Context A, Context B, or a new context"],"assumptions":["Blocking is an order STATE, not a rejection — the order is persisted but held","All-or-nothing reservation per order (no partial fulfillment)","Reservations live in or near Context A since they directly constrain StockLevel","A circuit breaker or fallback policy will be needed but is out of scope for this analysis"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The feature request says 'automatically block it if stock is insufficient' but does not specify whether this is a synchronous check during order placement (coupling the two contexts at write time) or an asynchronous process that transitions the order to 'blocked' after the fact. These have radically different architectural, consistency, and availability implications.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Sales Team","Customers"],"options":["A) Synchronous: Order placement calls Warehouse context, blocks inline. Simple but creates availability coupling.","B) Asynchronous: Order is accepted tentatively, then a domain event triggers a reservation attempt. If it fails, order transitions to Blocked. Decoupled but introduces a window where orders appear accepted but aren't.","C) Saga/Process Manager: Order enters 'Pending Stock Check' state, saga orchestrates the reservation, then transitions to Confirmed or Blocked. Most correct but most complex."],"recommendation":"Option C (Saga) is the most domain-faithful. Option B is acceptable as a pragmatic starting point. Option A should be rejected — it violates bounded-context autonomy and creates a single point of failure."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Real-time stock available indicator' is ambiguous. True real-time would require the sales UI to subscribe to warehouse state changes, which either couples the contexts or requires a dedicated read-model projection. The acceptable staleness (sub-second? 30 seconds? 5 minutes?) fundamentally changes the architecture.","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Team","Engineering"],"options":["A) Event-driven projection updated on every stock change — near-real-time (seconds of lag)","B) Polling-based cache refreshed every N seconds","C) On-demand query to warehouse context per product view"],"recommendation":"Option A (event-driven projection). It provides near-real-time freshness without synchronous coupling. The sales UI reads from a local projection, never from Context A directly."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"Who owns the Stock Reservation aggregate? It sits at the boundary between Warehouse and Sales. If it lives in Warehouse, Sales must request reservations via commands. If it lives in a new 'Order Fulfillment' context, it mediates between both. This ownership decision affects the entire integration architecture.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Warehouse Team","Sales Team"],"options":["A) Reservation owned by Context A (Warehouse) — Sales sends ReserveStock commands","B) New bounded context 'Inventory Allocation' owns reservations and mediates","C) Reservation owned by Context B (Sales) — Sales reads stock and manages its own reservations"],"recommendation":"Option A for simplicity or Option B for long-term correctness. Option C is rejected — Sales should not own stock-related aggregates as it leads to invariant violations."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Any implementation that has Context B (Sales) directly querying Context A's StockLevel table or aggregate to make order acceptance decisions","business_rule_broken":"BR-5: Bounded context autonomy. Direct coupling creates race conditions, availability dependency, and makes both contexts impossible to evolve independently."},{"violation":"Any implementation that treats the 'stock available' indicator as a guarantee used in order acceptance logic","business_rule_broken":"BR-4: The indicator is a read-model projection, eventually consistent. Using it as a write-path decision input creates a consistency hole."},{"violation":"Any implementation that allows two concurrent orders to both succeed when combined quantity exceeds ATP","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 and BR-3: StockLevel invariant and reservation-based ATP calculation. Overselling is a hard constraint violation."},{"violation":"Any implementation that creates reservations with no expiry or release mechanism","business_rule_broken":"BR-6: Without expiry, abandoned or failed orders permanently lock inventory, leading to phantom stock-outs."}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A feature request to integrate Warehouse Management (Context A) and Sales/Order Management (Context B) by blocking orders when stock is insufficient and showing real-time stock availability. This is a classic cross-bounded-context coupling request that fundamentally changes the autonomy of both contexts.","bounded_context":"Cross-context integration between Warehouse Management and Sales/Order Management.
